did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (EfficientNetB4, DenseNet-121, and EfficientNetV2) integrated with triplet attention and residual connections to classify skin lesions from the ISIC 2019 dataset. The primary task was skin lesion classification, specifically addressing melanoma and other skin cancers.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the training and evaluation of deep learning models (EfficientNetB4, DenseNet-121, and EfficientNetV2) with triplet attention and residual connections on the ISIC 2019 dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (accuracy, BMA, F1-score) and compares results with existing works, which is characteristic of a research study rather than a review.

title: Enhancing Skin Lesion Classification with Triplet Attention-Integrated Deep Learning Models
journal: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin lesion classification, particularly for melanoma, by integrating triplet attention and residual connections into pretrained deep learning models. The researchers evaluated EfficientNetB4, DenseNet-121, and EfficientNetV2 on a subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset, employing focal loss to mitigate class imbalance. The models were trained using an 80-10-10 stratified split with extensive data augmentation and normalized inputs. Evaluation metrics included accuracy, balanced multiclass accuracy (BMA), and F1-score. The ResTA-EfficientNetV2 model achieved the highest performance with 85.76% accuracy, 86.67% BMA, and 83.45% F1-score. The results demonstrate that combining triplet attention and residual mechanisms significantly enhances classification accuracy compared to baseline models and existing works.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the selected subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset with 4,000 samples used for classification.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions using a subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset. The models predict discrete diagnostic labels for eight specific lesion classes. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, balanced multiclass accuracy (BMA), F1-score, precision, and recall. The primary model (ResTA-EfficientNetV2) achieved 85.76% accuracy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Dataset (Subset)
datasets_used.description: A subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset, which combines the BCN20000, HAM10000, and MSK datasets. The original dataset consists of 25,331 dermoscopic images from various locations and preprocessing techniques. The study used a carefully selected subset to address class imbalance, excluding the 'Unknown' (UNK) category. Images are in 24-bit JPEG format.

use.training: Used for training the deep learning models (EfficientNetB4, DenseNet-121, EfficientNetV2) with an 80-10-10 stratified split.
use.validation: Used for validation with an 80-10-10 stratified split.
use.testing: Used for testing with an 80-10-10 stratified split.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the dataset into training, validation, and testing subsets while maintaining class distribution.
To mitigate class imbalance by focusing on harder-to-classify examples during training.
To address class imbalance by computing weights for each class using scikit-learn's compute class weight function.
To evaluate model performance by analyzing true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for each class.
To prevent overfitting by stopping training if validation loss does not improve for 15 epochs.
To dynamically adjust the learning rate based on validation loss plateauing.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported accuracy with a 95% confidence interval (84.04%, 87.47%) but did not perform statistical hypothesis tests to compare models.

ISIC 2019 Dataset (Subset).experiment: Skin Lesion Classification (8 classes: MEL, NV, BCC, AK, BKL, DF, VASC, SCC)
ISIC 2019 Dataset (Subset).notes: ResTA-EfficientNetV2 outperformed other models and existing works on this subset with respect to BMA and F1-score. The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (p-values) but provided a 95% confidence interval for the accuracy of the best model.

architecture_used.main_model: EfficientNetB4, DenseNet-121, and EfficientNetV2 integrated with Triplet Attention and Residual Connections (ResTA variants)
architecture_used.pretraining: EfficientNetB4 pretrained on ImageNet; EfficientNetV2 pretrained on ImageNet-21K and fine-tuned on ImageNet-1K; DenseNet-121 pretrained on ImageNet

architecture_details.attention_mechanism: Triplet Attention (computes attention maps along channel-height, channel-width, and height-width views using MaxPool and AvgPool, followed by shared convolution and sigmoid activation)
architecture_details.residual_connection: Added between original feature embedding and classifier head output; formula: y = F(x) + βWr(x)
architecture_details.classifier_head: Linear layers with BatchNorm1d, ReLU, and Dropout(0.2); Input features projected to 512, then to 8 classes

training_details.batch_size: 32 (effective); per-iteration batch size 16 with gradient accumulation for EfficientNetB4 due to memory constraints
training_details.loss_function: Focal Loss (gamma = 2) with class weights computed via scikit-learn
training_details.early_stopping: Patience of 15 epochs based on validation loss
data_augmentation: Random Affine Transformations (translation up to 20%, shearing up to 10%)
training_details.normalization: Channel-wise mean and standard deviation computed from dataset batches; pixel values scaled to [0, 1]

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing to 512 × 512 pixels for flexibility in cropping
Random Resized Crop to 380 × 380 pixels with a scale range of 90-100%
Random Affine Transformations including translations up to 20% and shearing up to 10 degrees
Color Jitter with brightness adjustments of ±15%
Tensor conversion with pixel values scaled to the [0, 1] range
Channel-wise normalization using dataset-specific mean and standard deviation computed from all batches
Stratified sampling for 80-10-10 train/validation/test split to maintain class distribution
Exclusion of the 'Unknown' (UNK) category from the dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were resized to 512 × 512 pixels to allow flexibility for random cropping.
Random cropping with an output size of 380 × 380 pixels and a scale range between 90% and 100% of the original resized area.
Images were rotated within a range of ±20 degrees.
Included small translations (up to 20% of image dimensions) and shearing (up to 10 degrees).
Random brightness adjustments of up to ±15% to simulate different lighting conditions.

conclusions: Integrating triplet attention and residual connections into pretrained deep learning models significantly enhances skin lesion classification performance compared to baseline models without these mechanisms.

The ResTA-EfficientNetV2 model achieved the highest performance among the tested architectures, with an accuracy of 85.76%, balanced multiclass accuracy (BMA) of 86.67%, and F1-score of 83.45% on the ISIC 2019 dataset subset.
The proposed method outperformed existing works on the same dataset subset with respect to BMA and F1-score.

The ResTA-EfficientNetV2 model demonstrated robust classification capabilities across eight skin lesion classes, with an area under the curve (AUC) of >= 0.97 for all classes and a micro-average precision-recall value of 93.00%.

The study confirms that using a lightweight pretrained model combined with triplet attention and residual connections provides high classification performance for skin lesions, aiding in early detection of melanoma and other skin cancers.
important_remarks: The study uses a heavily curated subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset (15,934 images) rather than the full dataset, which limits the generalizability of the results to the broader, more imbalanced real-world distribution of skin lesions.

The dataset retains significant class imbalance within the subset, with Melanocytic Nevus (NV) having 4,000 samples while Dermatofibroma (DF) has only 239, necessitating the use of focal loss and class weights to mitigate bias.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the significance of performance improvements over baseline models, relying instead on point estimates and confidence intervals.

The research is limited to a single dataset (ISIC 2019 subset) without external validation on independent cohorts, raising concerns about potential overfitting to specific image characteristics or acquisition protocols present in the ISIC data.

The reported accuracy of the best model (ResTA-EfficientNetV2) is 85.76%, which is notably lower than some cited prior works on the same subset (e.g., Modified GoogleNet at 94.92%), suggesting that the comparison metrics (BMA and F1-score) may not fully capture the nuances of performance differences or that the subset selection affects comparability.
The study excludes the 'Unknown' (UNK) category from the original ISIC 2019 dataset, which may limit the clinical applicability of the model for cases where diagnosis is uncertain or ambiguous.
